coredata.dc:title: In-situ green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for bioimaging and TiO2 nanoparticles@nitrogen-doped carbon composite for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Perumal, Suguna
Vinodh, Rajangam
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: As an efficient photocatalytic titanium dioxide nanoparticles@nitrogen-doped carbon (TiO2 NPs@C) nanocomposite and bright fluorescent with good biocompatible nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized at once by an affordable hydrothermal method. Physicochemical properties of the synthesized materials were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and elemental mapping analysis, high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The photocatalytic efficiency of synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was evaluated in the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under UV-light irradiation. The degradation efficiency of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite is about 90% when extending the irradiation time to 40 min, the rate constant is 5 times higher than that of bare TiO2 NPs. The augmentation of photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was attributed to the synergistic effect between TiO2 NPs and graphene-like carbon within the nanocomposite. The recycling studies were conducted and the photodegradation efficiency of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite did not show any significant changes up to three cycles, suggesting that the synthesized photocatalyst has good repeatability and the considerable stability. In addition, the carbon derived from a natural green source possesses the hydroxyl and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface which resulting TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite with high photocatalytic activity. Also, the obtained N-CDs displays almost zero toxicity towards the Candida albicans cells even at high concentrations which can be utilized as a powerful in-vitro label-free fluorescent probe for live cell imaging. Based on their bright with stable fluorescence and cellular imaging with good biocompatibility, N-CDs would offer a great potential for a wide range of applications in the biomedical and clinical applications in the near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peach fruit
Titanium dioxide
Carbon dots
Methylene blue
Photocatalytic degradation
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of bare TiO2 NPs, N-CDs, pristine carbon and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposites
Characterization of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs, pristine carbon and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite
Photocatalytic degradation of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite
Characterizations and properties of the obtained N-CDs
Cell viability and live cell imaging analysis of the obtained N-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Communication and Information Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was partially supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation ( NRF ) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science and Technology ( 2012M3A7B4049675 ) and the Priority Research Centers Program ( 2014R1A6A1031189 ). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation ( NRF ) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology ( MSIT ) of Korea government ( 2017R1C1B5076345 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite.
FESEM images with different magnification (a–d), elemental mapping of (e) carbon, (f) oxygen, (g) nitrogen and (h) corresponding EDX spectrum of the synthesized pristine carbon.

FESEM images with different magnification (a–d), elemental mapping of carbon (e), oxygen (f), nitrogen (g), Titanium (h) and corresponding EDX spectrum (i) of the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite.
HRTEM images with different magnification (a–e) and SAED pattern (f) of the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite.

(a) XPS survey scan spectrum, (b), (c), (d) and (e) XPS high-resolution spectra of C1s, N1s, Ti2p and O1s for the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite. ATR-FTIR spectrum of (f) the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite.

UV–vis spectrum of MB degradation in the presence of (a) TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite and (b) bare TiO2 NPs depends on irradiation time; (c) MB absorbance in the presence of pristine carbon at varying the rest time; (d) MB degradation (%) at different irradiation time and (e) irradiation time dependent first order kinetics plot of ln (At/A0) for MB degradation in the presence of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite and bare TiO2 NPs; (f) plausible photocatalytic mechanism for the MB degradation in presence of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite.

(a) MB degradation for the recycling experiments of the TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite (inset (a): UV–Vis absorbance spectra of aqueous MB solution after degradation about 40 min up to three cycles); (b) XRD pattern, (c) FESEM image and (d) HRTEM image of recycled TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite (after 3rd cycle of photodegradation on aqueous MB).
(a), (b) HRTEM images with different magnification, (c) AFM topographic image and (d) XRD pattern of the obtained N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence excitation spectra (inset (a): photographs of diluted N-CDs aqueous solution under daylight and UV-light with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm), and (b) excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the obtained N-CDs.
(a) Cell growth and (b) redox activity of the obtained N-CDs against Candida albicans.
Confocal fluorescent microscopy images of C. albicans cells in the presence and absence of resulted N-CDs for 2 h and 6 h incubation time at the different excitation wavelength.
Illustration for the synthesis procedure of N-CDs and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: Detailed instrumentation methods; FESEM images with EDX spectrum of bare TiO2 NPs; ATR-FTIR spectrum and excitation-dependent normalized fluorescence spectra of the obtained N-CDs.

head.ce:title: In-situ green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for bioimaging and TiO2 nanoparticles@nitrogen-doped carbon composite for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Hanseo University, Seosan 360-706, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an efficient photocatalytic titanium dioxide nanoparticles@nitrogen-doped carbon (TiO2 NPs@C) nanocomposite and bright fluorescent with good biocompatible nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized at once by an affordable hydrothermal method. Physicochemical properties of the synthesized materials were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and elemental mapping analysis, high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The photocatalytic efficiency of synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was evaluated in the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under UV-light irradiation. The degradation efficiency of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite is about 90% when extending the irradiation time to 40 min, the rate constant is 5 times higher than that of bare TiO2 NPs. The augmentation of photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was attributed to the synergistic effect between TiO2 NPs and graphene-like carbon within the nanocomposite. The recycling studies were conducted and the photodegradation efficiency of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite did not show any significant changes up to three cycles, suggesting that the synthesized photocatalyst has good repeatability and the considerable stability. In addition, the carbon derived from a natural green source possesses the hydroxyl and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface which resulting TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite with high photocatalytic activity. Also, the obtained N-CDs displays almost zero toxicity towards the Candida albicans cells even at high concentrations which can be utilized as a powerful in-vitro label-free fluorescent probe for live cell imaging. Based on their bright with stable fluorescence and cellular imaging with good biocompatibility, N-CDs would offer a great potential for a wide range of applications in the biomedical and clinical applications in the near future.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the rapid development of industry and usage of hazardous organic dyes has increased environmental issues such as pollution including water pollution, air pollution and soil pollution, toxicity and carcinogenic effect all over the world [1–3]. To reduce the serious problem of these pollutants, increase the scientific efforts on this issues. Particularly, to avoid the water pollution, the major pollutants including hazardous organic dyes and heavy metals, should be removed from industrial waste-water before being released into the environment. In general, adsorption, chemical precipitation, sedimentation, ion-exchange, electrochemical and photocatalytic methods are used to reduce the water pollution [4]. Among them, photocatalytic degradation is an energy-saving, simple and highly efficient with the eco-friendly method and was used in the disposal of sewage [5,6]. Particularly, methylene blue (MB) is one of the colored organic compounds (organic dyes) and it is the most commonly used materials for dying cotton, silk, and wood. However, it can harm the living organism due to their toxicity, carcinogenicity and so forth. Hence, the removal of MB from industrial effluents is considered one of the environmental challenges in the recent years [7–10]. Various semiconducting metal oxide nanoparticles as photocatalysts have been widely explored to the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants such as MB and so on [11–15]. Among the photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles have great attention by the scientific research workers due to their high photosensitivity, stability, nontoxicity, chemical inertness, dispersion property, low cost, and availability in abundance [16–21]. However, TiO2 nanoparticles have some limitations such as high electron-hole pair recombination, wide band-gap energy, aggregation at high concentration, and low surface area on photodegradation of organic dyes [22,23]. In order to increase the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanoparticles, graphene-like materials have attached with TiO2 nanoparticles. These carbon-based TiO2 nanoparticles have been widely used for the successful degradation of organic dyes under the irradiation of UV-light. The high photosensitivity of carbon-based TiO2 nanoparticles due to their synergetic effect between TiO2 and carbon. The green chemical assisted hydrothermal process is simple and eco-friendly methods compared to the other synthesis method of carbon-based photocatalyst as well as carbon-based TiO2 NPs nanocomposite [24]. In this context, novel titanium dioxide nanoparticles@nitrogen-doped carbon (TiO2 NPs@C) nanocomposite are established through facile green synthesis route for improvement of photosensitization.

On another hand, fluorescence-based materials have received much attention recently because of their excellent properties such as sensitivity, short response time, and low cost [25]. Thus, fluorescence materials are used in wide range of applications such as fluorescent probes in sensors and imaging [26]. Among the nanomaterials, quantum dots are one the fluorescent materials attracted the researcher much in recent years. However, quantum dots are expensive, are difficult to synthesize, and are toxic thus limits the applications in sensors and bioimaging. Therefore, researchers focused to develop new fluorescent nanomaterials with low toxicity and for a wide range of applications [27]. Carbon dots (CDs) are one kind of newly discovered fluorescent nanomaterials is popular due to their unique optical properties, biocompatible, great aqueous stability, and facile synthesis [28–34]. CDs and heteroatom (N, S, P, S and etc.) doped CDs have been synthesized using various methods, acidic oxidation, electrochemical synthesis, hydrothermal synthesis, laser ablation, arc discharge, and microwave-assisted synthesis [35–39]. Among the methods preparation of CDs and heteroatom doped CDs using eco-friendly biomass such as fruits and leaves via hydrothermal method is highly encouraged because of the green chemistry approach and are widely used [40].

In the present work, we approach a facile one-step synthesize of nitrogen-doped CDs and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite using peach fruit extract as carbon precursor via hydrothermal method. Comparing to reported methods to prepared heteroatom doped CDs and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite, this approach has various advantages like simple, rapid and environmentally friendly due to peach fruit is the most abundant biosource in nature. Also, the use of biosources such as peach fruit may represent an important strategy to reduce the final cost of producing the carbon nanomaterials with functional groups. The satisfactory amount of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups over the CDs enhance the fluorescence properties (quantum yield) with biocompatibility, allowing its potential application in bioimaging and biomedical. The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was explored toward the photodegradation of MB as a model of pollutants in water, with the aim to evaluate the potential application of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite in environmental remediation. In order to find the extraordinary photocatalytic activity of synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite, the dye degradation efficiency (%) and rate constant (k) of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite on MB were compared to bare TiO2 NPs.

The peach fruit was procured from the Kyungpook National University Campus, Daegu, Republic of Korea. Titanium (IV) isopropoxide, aqueous ammonia (25%), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), quinine sulfate, sodium azide, iodonitrotetrazolium salt (INT), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), ethanol, MB, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Republic of Korea. The yeast strains Candida albicans (C. albicans-KCTC-11282) was procured from the Korean Cell Type Culture Collection (KCTC), Seoul, Republic of Korea, maintained in 10% v/v glycerol stocks, and stored at −80 °C until further use. Overnight cultures in malt extract (ME) broth were used for the cellular imaging experiments. All the chemicals were used as received. The double distilled (DD) water was used for the preparation of samples throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bare TiO2 NPs, N-CDs, pristine carbon and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The TiO2 NPs were synthesized from titanium (IV) isopropoxide by the simple sol-gel method as per our previous report [41]. In a typical synthesis procedure, 50 mL of absolute alcohol and 50 mL of DI water was taken into a beaker and mixed well. An 18 mL of titanium (IV) isopropoxide in 40 mL of anhydrous ethanol was added dropwise to the above mixture under vigorous stirring and then stirring was extended for 60 min at room temperature. 1 M H2SO4 was added dropwise into reaction mixture until it reaches the pH 3.5 and then stirred for additional 60 min. After completion, the final mixture was centrifuged, washed with DD water several time and then the residue was dried at 70 °C for 5 h in a hot air oven. The dried material was calcined at 400 °C for 4 h in order to remove the water molecules and the hydroxyl group over the surface and core of the synthesized materials. The final material was denoted as bare TiO2 NPs and was stored in a glass vial for further use. The N-CDs, as well as TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite, were simultaneously synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Typically, 30 mL of peach juice and 200 mg of TiO2 NPs were taken into a beaker followed by the addition of 1 mL aqueous ammonia and mixed well. This starting mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, subsequently heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the completion of the hydrothermal process, the final mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature, filtered, and washed with DD water followed by ethanol. The thoroughly washed residue was dried at 70 °C for overnight and the obtained dark brown solid denoted as TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite. The as-received by-product (filtrate) shows a bright blue fluorescence under UV-light with a wavelength of about 365 nm which denoted as N-CDs. The received bright fluorescence N-CDs was further applied for cellular imaging under various excitation wavelength and the TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was utilized for the organic dye (MB) degradation under irradiation of UV-light. The synthesis procedure of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite with N-CDs is clearly demonstrated in Scheme 1 . A similar procedure was adopted for the synthesis of pristine carbon. Typically, 30 mL of peach juice hydrothermally heated at 180 °C for 12 h and resulting product (residue) is denoted as pristine carbon. The synthesized materials were characterized by X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) and elemental mapping analysis, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) techniques. The detailed instrumentation methods have described in ESI.

The photocatalytic degradation was examined using MB as an organic dye, degradation measurements were carried out in the neutral aqueous medium at room temperature under UV-light irradiation. For this experiments, the absorbance intensity of organic dyes was fixed around 1.5. Typically, 20 mg of the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was mixed into a 50 mL of aqueous MB dye in a glass beaker. The suspension was placed at 100 mm distance from UV light source and was irradiated with mild stirring until the decoloration of aqueous MB dye. A 2 mL of suspensions were collected for every 10 min time intervals and absorbance was measured until zero value attained by the UV–vis spectrophotometer. The similar procedure was adopted for the MB dye degradation in presence of bare TiO2 NPs. The percentage and rate constant (k) of MB dye degradation were calculated in the presence of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite. The TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was recovered after the photocatalytic degradation of neutral aqueous MB dye under UV-light irradiation. Typically, the degraded mixture of MB solution was centrifuged and then received residue was dried at 70 °C for overnight in a hot air oven under atmospheric condition. The dried TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was again used for the photocatalytic degradation of neutral aqueous MB dye under UV-light irradiation up to three cycles (in each cycle, the same sample was placed into a fresh aqueous solution of MB for 40 min under UV-light irradiation). From the final cycle, the recovered TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was characterized by various physicochemical techniques such as XRD, FESEM and HRTEM analysis to determine the structure of the recycled materials.

The growth inhibition activity of the N-CDs against the C. albicans (test pathogen) was determined by a broth microdilution method. Typically, the test pathogen (6 × 106 CFU/mL) was inoculated into 1 mL of ME medium in culture tubes (10 mL) previously adjusted with various initial concentrations of the test agent (ranges from 3.12 to 100 μL). The culture tubes were statically incubated at 28 °C for 24 h. The optical density (OD) of the culture was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (JASCO, UV530, Japan) at regular time intervals. The average percentage of cell growth ± SD of triplicate values was calculated. The C. albicans was grown in malt extract broth (MEB, BD science, CA) at 28 °C for overnight. The C. albicans cell suspension was washed twice and re-suspended in PBS (pH 7.4). The cells (1 × 106 CFU/mL) were mixed with 10 μL of N-CDs in DD water and incubated statically at 28 °C for 6 h for the staining of the cells. At specific time points, the cell suspension (1 mL) was aseptically pelleted by centrifugation (6000 rpm, 10 min) and the pellet was resuspended in 0.1 mL PBS solution. A 10 μL of the culture suspension was spotted onto an agarose pad which was mounted on a microscopic glass slide. Fluorescence imaging was performed using a Nikon Eclipse 80 I laser scanning confocal microscope and observed in deep UV and UV regions (blue) with excitation at 248 and 408 nm, respectively.

The cytotoxicity of the synthesized N-CDs was calculated by determining the cellular redox activity of C. albicans using iodonitrotetrazolium salt (INT). Typically, the C. albicans (5 × 106 CFU/mL) was prepared in 10 mL of pre-filtered PBS, subsequently added with a concentration gradient of the test agent prepared in water. A 50 μL of 10 μM INT solution was added to each solution and the glass tubes were incubated at 28 °C for 6 h in a dark place. 1 mL of cell suspension was aseptically withdrawn at 60 min interval into Eppendorf and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min. The cell pellets were extracted with 0.5 mL of DMSO and the extracted red INT-formazan product was spectrophotometrically measured at a wavelength of 545 nm. Sodium azide (50 μM) was used as a positive control (PC). Each experiment was repeated twice and the average ± SD value of respiratory activity was calculated. The percentage redox activity of cells (percentage of cell viability) was determined by the following equation (1):
(1)
Cell
redox
activity =
A b
s
( S )
− A b
s
( B )
A b
s
( C )
− A b
s
( B )
×100
where Abs
(S)
, Abs
(B) , and Abs
(C) are the absorbance of the sample, blank, and control, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs, pristine carbon and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the one-step synthesize of N-CDs as well as TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite from peach juice using the hydrothermal method. In order to compare the properties of prepared TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite with pristine carbon, the later one was prepared without TiO2 NPs by adopting the same method as that earlier one. The synthesized bare TiO2 NPs, TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite, and pristine carbon were characterized by various methods. Fig. S1 shows the FESEM images and corresponding EDX pattern of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs. It can be seen spongy-like morphology due to aggregation of individual tiny TiO2 NPs with somewhat spherical nature and the elemental composition was revealed by EDX spectrum. The EDX data endorse that the samples are composed of Ti and O elements [42,43]. Moreover, FESEM with EDX analysis shows that there is the desired composition of Ti and O without any other impurities. Fig. 1 (a) shows the XRD pattern of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite. The peaks from XRD reveals clearly the presence of pure anatase TiO2 NPs and match well with the JCPDS card no. 84-1285 [41]. In addition, the peaks at 25.5 and 41.6° corresponds to C (002) and C (100) planes, respectively [44]. The planes of C (002) and C (100) confirms the existence of graphitic structure in TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposites along with TiO2 NPs with an acceptable degree of graphitization. The average size of the anchored TiO2 NPs on the carbon sheets was around 5.2 nm with a d-spacing value of 0.35 nm based on (101) plane of anatase TiO2 NPs. The Raman spectrum of the TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 1(b). A broad TiO2 Raman peak is seen around 600–800 cm−1 additionally, composite shows two peaks at about 1350 cm−1 (D band) and 1597 cm−1 (G band) as observed in the spectrum, which attributed to the defected (disordered) carbon and graphitic (ordered) carbon, respectively in the typical graphene structure [45,46]. The intensity ratio of D and G band (ID/IG) is ca. 0.35 which demonstrates the resulting TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite possess an adequate degree of graphitization. The XRD pattern and Raman spectrum confirm the presence of TiO2 NPs and graphene-like carbon structure in TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite without any impurities.

The morphological features of the prepared TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite and pristine carbon were studied based on the FESEM with EDX and HRTEM with SAED measurements (Figs. 2–4
). Fig. 2 shows the FESEM images and elemental mapping with EDX spectrum of synthesized pristine carbon, smooth surface is observed in Fig. 2 (a–d). Aggregated structure of pristine carbon is clear from Fig. 2 (a), however, the higher magnification confirms the folded structure of pristine carbon from Fig. 2 (b and c). The elemental mapping of pristine carbon proves the existence of carbon and oxygen element abundantly in the peach extract with a trace amount of nitrogen moiety (Fig. 2(e–g)). Further, the presence of carbon and oxygen element was supported by EDX spectrum (Fig. 2(h)).

The FESEM images show that the carbon surface is completely covered by an individual TiO2 NPs and homogenous distribution with almost spherical shaped TiO2 NPs can be seen in Fig. 3(a–d). The high-resolution images, Fig. 3 (c) exhibits the presence of TiO2 particles with a spherical shape. In addition, the surface roughness of the carbon slightly increased for TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite due to the presence of TiO2 NPs on the surface of the carbon. The elemental mapping of nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 3 (e–h) and proves the existence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and titanium elements. The homogenous distribution of TiO2 NPs on the carbon structure further evident by the mapping. The nitrogen element because of the addition of ammonia during the synthesis of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite. Further EDX spectral analysis confirms the elemental composition of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite (Fig. 3(i)). To elucidate the morphology of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite, HRTEM measurement was carried out. The HRTEM images (Fig. 4) is in good agreement with its corresponding FESEM images (Fig. 3). The HRTEM images (Fig. 4 (a–c)) shows the existence of individual spherical TiO2 NPs were joined together to form clusters of TiO2 NPs on the carbon structure. However, homogenous distribution of TiO2 NPs on carbon structure can be seen from HRTEM images. The high-resolution images (Fig. 4 (d and e)) reveals the TiO2 NPs are spherical and are about 5 ± 0.5 nm in diameter. Lattice fringes are clearly seen in the high-resolution image with an interlayer distance of 0.35 nm which match well with XRD results. SAED pattern (Fig. 4(f)) shows the diffraction rings of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite clearly evidence that the TiO2 NPs are crystalline in nature with smaller size and coincides with the XRD result.

The surface chemical analysis of the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was carried out using XPS. Fig. 5 (a) shows the survey spectrum of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite, the existence of Ti, N, O, and C elements confirms the presence of TiO2 NPs on carbon structure. The atomic percentage of N is around 8% in the TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite and which enhance the activity of their materials. Fig. 5 (b–e) shows the core level XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, Ti2p, and O1s for TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite. The deconvoluted C1s level shows four peaks at 284.28, 284.78, 285.68 eV, and 286.98 corresponding to the CC, CC, COH/CN, and COC/OCO bonds, respectively [47]. In addition to these four peaks, a satellite peak at 291.68 eV was observed. Deconvolution of N1s spectrum showed only three peaks centered at 398.98, 399.88, and 401.28 eV attributed to CNC (Pyridinic N), NH (pyrrolic N), and C3 N (Graphitic N) groups, respectively [48,49]. This suggest that the N atoms present in the frame work of carbon. The high-resolution Ti2p spectrum showed two peaks centered at 458.58 and 464.28 eV which are due to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, respectively. This match well with a report published about TiO2 NPs [50,51]. The O1s level deconvolution revealed the existence of three functionalities, TiOTi/COC/COH, CO/TiOH/OCO, and HOH with binding energy 530.08, 532.18, and 536.08 eV, respectively [52]. It is noteworthy that the high-resolution spectra gave an unambiguous picture about the existing carbon structure (from carbon level), oxygen and nitrogen functional groups from peach extract and TiO2 NPs in the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite. Other than XPS, ATR-FTIR also used to confirms the existence of functional groups in TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite (Fig. 5(f)). The broad hydroxyl group (OH) stretching vibrations due to the functional groups or physically adsorbed water molecules were observed around 3250 cm−1 [53]. The CH as-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations were observed at 2928 and 2860 cm−1, respectively [54,55]. Several characteristic absorption bands of oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups were observed for TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite. The absorption peaks included 1690 cm−1 for CO stretching vibration, 1606 cm−1 for CC group vibration from sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon structure, 1438 cm−1 for CN group, 1368 cm−1 for phenolic COH stretching vibration, and the absorption peaks between 1282 and 1026 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO, and epoxy (COC)/CN functional groups [56,57]. In addition, the TiO/TiOTi framework vibration observed at 580 cm−1. The XPS and ATR-FTIR results confirm the presence of oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups with TiO2 NPs on synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of the bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was evaluated by the photocatalytic decomposition MB dye under UV-light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 6 . The absorption intensity of MB is decreased in presence of TiO2 NPs as well as TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite with increasing irradiation time (Fig. 6 (a and b)). The decrease in absorption intensity of MB in presence of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite are drastically changed (Fig. 6(a)), whereas the change in absorption intensity in presence of bare TiO2 NPs slightly changes (Fig. 6(b)). In order to find the better degradation efficiency of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite, adsorption study also carried out for pristine carbon derived from peach juice on MB dye in the absence of UV-light (under dark atmosphere). The change in absorption intensity in presence of pristine carbon are insignificant (Fig. 6(c)). Hence, the degradation efficiency of synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite is compared to only bare TiO2 NPs. The degradation efficiency of synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite is about 90% when extending the irradiation time to 40 min, the efficiency is much higher than the bare TiO2 NPs which is about 39% shown in Fig. 6(d). Reactions followed pseudo-first order kinetics and rates of kinetic resolute from the linear portion of the reaction profiles (Fig. 6(e)) [58,59]. The rate constant (k) of the synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite is ca. 0.0593 min−1 (9.88 × 10−4 s−1) with the R2 value of 0.987, which is the greater value than the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs on MB degradation (0.0118 min−1 (1.97 × 10−4 s−1), R2 = 0.924). The rate constant of synthesized TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite is 5 times higher than that of the rate constant of bare TiO2 NPs on MB degradation. The augmentation of photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was attributed to the synergistic effect between TiO2 NPs and graphitic carbon. In addition, the graphitic carbon derived from a natural green source possesses the hydroxyl and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface which resulting TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite with higher photocatalytic activity. A plausible photodegradation mechanism of MB in the presence of TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite has been proposed based on the photocatalytic studies and shown in Fig. 6(f). During the photocatalytic process, the MB dye in water converted into degraded product (DP) including some mineral acids. The detailed mechanism as adopted in our previous report [41]. These result strongly exposed that the synthesized TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite is a potential candidate for photocatalytic degradation of MB with a high rate of kinetic under UV-light irradiation in a neutral aqueous medium.

The recycling studies were followed with TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite in order to find the catalyst stability over a large period of exploitation and the catalyst stability is an important feature of emerging for an industrial photocatalysis process. During the photocatalysis process, the physicochemical properties of the TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite should not change for good photocatalyst. In the present case, Fig. 7 (a) shows the photodegradation efficiency of TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite (photocatalyst) on MB dye suggest reuse of photocatalyst in the photocatalytic process and showed no significant changes up to three cycles. This suggest that the photocatalytic activity has good reusability, considerable stability, and being of significance for practical use of the photocatalyst [60,61]. The minute decrement of photodegradation efficiency caused by the material (TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite) loss during the recovering procedure. In addition, the recycled TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite showed no apparent changes in the physicochemical properties such as structural and chemical compositions (XRD pattern, FESEM image and TEM image) compared to as-synthesized TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite (Figs. 1, 3 and 4) even after three cycles of reuse which is shown in Fig. 7(b-d). This study indicated that the structure of the TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite was not affected or chemically transformed to other organic compounds during photocatalytic process. Hence, the TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite not only has good reproducibility photocatalytic activity towards photocatalytic degradation of MB dye under UV-light irradiation, which is of great significance for practical (industrial) applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and properties of the obtained N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As explained in Scheme 1, the filtrate from the synthesis of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite, labeled as N-CDs. The obtained N-CDs also were thoroughly examined by many physicochemical characterizations including HRTEM, atomic force microscopy (AFM), XRD, ATR-FTIR, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. HRTEM images and AFM topographic image of obtained N-CDs as shown in Fig. 8 . The HRTEM images clearly demonstrate the obtained N-CDs exhibit a uniform dispersion and a relatively narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 4 nm (Fig. 8(a)). Also, the HRTEM images indicate that the nanoparticles reveal crystalline features and showed lattice fringes with an interlayer distance of the N-CDs was 0.21 nm (Fig. 8(b)), in agreement with the interspacing of the (100) plane of the typical graphite (graphene) [62–65]. The results confirm the obtained N-CDs are a relatively high degree of graphitization. The AFM topographic image of the obtained N-CDs (Fig. 8(c)) shows the dot-like morphology with homogeneously and narrow size distribution as well as smaller in size [66].

In order to define the structure and components of the obtained N-CDs were characterized by XRD and FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 8(d) displays the XRD pattern of the obtained N-CDs, which indicate two peaks, the broad intense peak around 23.5° and weak peak at 42.6° are credited to the (002) and (100) planes for the typical graphene (graphitic carbon) structure, respectively and no other crystalline phases, which confirms that absence of any other impurities from the obtained host N-CDs. Also, the broad diffraction peak is might be due to the small size as well as acceptable graphitization of the N-CDs [67]. The d-spacing values of the N-CDs are 0.37 and 0.21 nm calculated using Bragg's law based on the C (002) and C (100) planes, respectively which match well with the calculated lattice spacing of N-CDs from HRTEM. However, the d-spacing value of N-CDs (0.37 nm) little deviated from the standard graphite or graphene (0.34 nm). The slightly higher d-spacing value of N-CDs indicating the little-disordered carbon (moderate crystallization/graphitization) which due to O-containing and N-containing functional groups in the carbon framework. The O-containing and N-containing functional groups were revealed by the ATR-FTIR spectroscopy analysis which is shown in Fig. S2. These functional groups on the CDs arising from the peach juice and aqueous ammonia, respectively. In the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs, the broad absorbance band at 3250 cm−1 indicates the surface OH stretching vibration of the carboxylic/surface hydroxyl functional groups and or physically adsorbed water molecules. The band at 2928 cm−1 is attributed to the as-symmetric stretching vibrations of CH bond [68]. A weak band around 1695 cm−1 is assigned to CO, a little amount of carboxylic acid present on the surface of the N-CDs. The sharp bands centered at 1576, 1400, 1320, and 1090 cm−1 are attributed to the carbogenic core of CC stretching vibration, stretching vibration of the CN bonds, stretching vibration of COH bonds, the formation of the CNC/COC bonds, respectively [69,70]. Hence, the FTIR affirming the presence of OH, CH, CO, CC, CN, COH, CNC and COC groups in the obtained N-CDs.

The optical properties of the obtained N-CDs were characterized by UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy and fluorescence excitation and emission spectroscopy techniques. Fig. 9 shows the UV–vis and fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs. In the UV–vis absorbance spectrum, a high intense maximum absorption band at 270 nm and a broad weak shoulder band at 306 nm were observed. The absorption at 270 nm is assigned to the π–π∗ transition of the in-plane sp2-hybridized honeycomb-like (CC) carbon structure. The second weak shoulder peak can be assigned to n–π∗ transition of the oxygen-containing or nitrogen-containing functional groups (CO/CN) in the carbon structure [71,72]. The aqueous N-CDs solution under excitation of UV light ((λex = 365 nm) emits bright blue color fluorescence while under daylight it does not emit light and shows pale yellow with transparent (inset of Fig. 9 (a)). This emits in bright light can be attributed to the presence of CDs. The fluorescence excitation spectra in Fig. 9 (a) shows an intense peak at 341 nm. Fig. 9 (b) shows the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra. In this spectra, an obvious change in emission spectra with varying excitation wavelength is clear. The emission spectra wavelength gradually increases from 410 to 480 nm with increase in excitation wavelength from 300 to 410 nm. This maximum emission observed at the excitation of 340 nm than gradually decreased with increase in excitation wavelength, in addition, a redshift is observed in emission spectrum at the higher excitation wavelength. The emission of obtained N-CDs was shifted towards the higher wavelength based on increasing of excitation wavelength which is clearly observed in the normalized fluorescence spectra (Fig. S3). These dramatic changes of the emission color (wavelength) as a function of the excitation wavelength are generally due to the different sizes of fluorescent species in the sample. Hence, we believed that the surface functional groups as well as size of species mainly control the fluorescence behaviour of the N-CDs [73,74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability and live cell imaging analysis of the obtained N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The zero toxicity of resulting materials should be considered for live cell imaging and bio-clinical applications. Hence, the cell viability (toxicity) test for the obtained N-CDs against live cells (C. albicans) was conducted prior to use the cell imaging application. The cell viability was examined by the time growth kinetic and in-vitro redox activity (respiratory activity). Fig. 10 (a) shows the time growth kinetics of C. albicans in the absence (control) and presence of N-CDs with varying the concentrations. The growth of C. albicans in the presence of N-CDs was similar that of the control and also insignificant growth difference at all the concentration of N-CDs. In case of positive control (PC, sodium azide treated N-CDs) exhibited significant reduction (>85%) in the cell growth. The modest reduction in the stationary phase growth beyond 8 h incubation time for all the concentration. The results suggested that the obtained N-CDs have insignificant growth hindrance on C. albicans which means nearing zero toxicity on live cells. Even though, the cell viability of N-CDs against C. albicans was further tested by a respiratory activity/redox activity. Fig. 10(b) shows the respiratory activity of C. albicans in the absence and presence of N-CDs by varying the concentrations. The respiratory activity of C. albicans in the presence N-CDs with different concentrations was closely similar that of control. Also, the respiratory activity increases with increasing the incubation time from 0 to 8 h which indicated the N-CDs did not prevent the respiratory activity of C. albicans. But the PC (50 μM) shows the almost absence of respiratory activity. The N-CDs displays the absence of cytotoxicity (good biocompatibility) towards the C. albicans cells even at high concentrations which can be utilized as a powerful in-vitro fluorescent probe for the detection of cells in the biological system.

The cellular imaging of N-CDs treated C. albicans and untreated C. albicans (control) was examined by fluorescence microscopy analysis which is shown in Fig. 11 . The C. albicans incubated with N-CDs (10 μg mL−1) for 2 h, exhibited an intense fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 286 (deep UV) and 408 nm (blue), and this autofluorescence intensity remained stable over the period of 6 h. In case of control, there is no autofluorescence. Hence, the results confirm the N-CDs were successfully internalized in the cytoplasm of cells and exhibited a strong fluorescence by the excitation. The durable and stable fluorescence of cell imaging is by the strong interaction between the N-CDs and nucleolus in the cytoplasm of cells. The strong interaction is due to hydrophilic nature of obtained N-CDs with the various functional groups includes oxygen and nitrogen [75]. Based on their bright with stable fluorescence and cellular imaging with good biocompatibility, N-CDs would offer a great potential for a wide-range of applications in the biomedical and clinical applications in the close future.

In this research, the hydrothermal process was employed to synthesize TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite and N-CDs at once as a photocatalyst and label-free fluorescent probe, respectively. The new TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite showed excellent photocatalytic performance on MB under irradiation of UV-light and the degradation efficiency (>90%) of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was 5 times higher than that of bare TiO2 NPs. The intensification of photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite was ascribed to the synergistic effect between TiO2 NPs and graphene-like carbon. Also, the carbon derived from a natural green source exhibits the oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface which is more hydroxyl and superoxide radicals produced and resulting in the efficient photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the recyclability study indicated that the structure of the TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite was not affected during photocatalytic process. Hence, the TiO2 NPs @C nanocomposite not only has good photocatalytic activity and also has a good reproducibility of photocatalytic degradation, which is of great significance for industrial applications. The obtained N-CDs displays the bright fluorescence with good biocompatibility towards the C. albicans cells and thus was utilized as a powerful fluorescent probe for live cell imaging without any further modification. Given the ideal results of this study, more research should be carried out on the use of TiO2 NPs@C nanocomposite for the degradation of MB in waste-water and N-CDs would offer a great potential for a wide-range of applications in the biomedical and clinical in the near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was partially supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675) and the Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government (2017R1C1B5076345).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.06.272.
